interpretations.interpretation: As part of a genealogical record, the audience would understand these lines as affirming a direct dynastic line from Jesse to King David and then to Solomon—i.e., that Solomon’s kingship (and right to rule) comes through David.

For a Jewish/early Christian audience, naming David “the king” and then Solomon would read as connecting the present/coming community to an important scriptural royal tradition tied to God’s promises to David.

The phrase “from/through the [woman] of Uriah” would likely prompt the audience to recall the well-known narrative of Uriah and David’s household; thus, they might infer an implicit allusion to Bathsheba (Uriah’s wife) and the broader story behind Solomon’s birth.

Because the text preserves the mention of Uriah, some listeners might interpret it as highlighting that God’s purposes work through ordinary, complicated human circumstances—even those involving wrongdoing, loss, or scandal—rather than presenting a purely idealized line of descent.

In a genealogical setting, the audience might also treat this as a legitimizing statement: Solomon is portrayed as connected to David even though the maternal reference is expressed indirectly (via “Uriah”), which may signal careful wording meant to connect royal lineage while referencing established scripture.
